title: Suturing — Basic
oneLiner: A practical skill for closing clean lacerations in ED/ward; primary intention healing reduces infection and scar; requires knowledge of technique, wound assessment, and timing.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Missed arterial injury:** "Pale, pulseless, cold, painful distal limb" — do not suture until you have confirmed distal pulse (Doppler) and no expanding haematoma.
- **Necrotising fasciitis:** "Crepitus + extreme pain out of proportion + systemic sepsis" — immediate surgical debridement, not closure.
- **Compound fracture or open joint:** "Visible bone, fat globules, air in joint on XR, or wound over a joint with hemarthrosis" — do not close; refer to orthopaedics.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible break in skin (laceration, incised wound, flap, puncture)
  - Bleeding (can be minimal or brisk)
  - Pain localised to wound
  - Mechanism: sharp (knife, glass, metal) vs blunt (tear, crush) vs bite (human/animal)
  - Time since injury (delayed closure >6h in non-facial wounds increases infection risk)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Wound location, length, depth, shape, edges (clean vs ragged)
  - Neurovascular status distal to wound: pulse, capillary refill, sensation (light touch, two-point discrimination), motor function (especially for hand/finger wounds)
  - Tendon function: test active flexion/extension against resistance
  - Foreign body: visible dirt, glass, metal; palpate for hard objects
  - Signs of infection at presentation: erythema, purulent discharge, lymphangitis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators
  - **Simple superficial laceration:** skin only, no deep structure involvement, clean wound
  - **Deep laceration with tendon injury:** patient cannot actively flex/extend digit (e.g., zone II flexor tendon injury)
  - **Laceration with nerve injury:** sensory loss distal to wound (e.g., digital nerve injury in finger laceration)
  - **Laceration with vascular injury:** pulsatile bleeding, haematoma, distal ischaemia
  - **Open fracture or joint:** bone visible, fat globules, air track on XR; joint effusion with haemarthrosis
  - **Puncture wound:** small entry but may track deep; high risk of retained foreign body or deep infection (e.g., nail)
  - **Human/animal bite:** crush injury, high bacterial load, high risk of infection (e.g., clenched fist injury)
- **Splitter:** "Ability to fully extend the finger against resistance" – if present, flexor tendon injury is unlikely; if absent, immediate senior referral.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Wound irrigation with copious normal saline (at least 250-500 mL) – use 18G cannula and 50 mL syringe for high pressure
  - Explore wound with sterile probe/gauze – assess depth, check for foreign body or tendon exposure
  - Check tetanus immunisation status (UK Green Book schedule)
- **Bloods:**
  - Not routine for simple suturing
  - If signs of infection, severe wound, or immunocompromise: FBC, CRP, wound swab for MC&S
  - If human/animal bite or immunocompromised: consider HIV, Hep B/C (if high risk)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** X-ray two views (AP + lateral) for any wound with suspected foreign body (glass, metal, gravel) or possible fracture
  - **Second-line:** Ultrasound/CT for radiolucent foreign bodies (wood, plastic)
- **Special tests:**
  - Remove tourniquet if used for haemostasis before closure (risk of ischaemia)
  - For hand wounds: test flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) and superficialis (FDS)
- **Never-miss test:** X-ray for foreign body in any wound from glass, metal, or gravel – omission leads to retained foreign body and chronic infection.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Achieve haemostasis: direct pressure for 5 min, elevate, use tourniquet only temporarily
  - Clean wound: irrigate with saline, remove visible debris, consider 1% povidone-iodine for contaminated wounds (but avoid in clean wounds)
  - Anaesthesia: local infiltration with 1% lignocaine (max dose 200 mg plain, 500 mg with adrenaline – **[check local policy]**) – use adrenaline with caution in end arteries (fingers, toes, penis, ear, nose)
  - Suture technique:
    - Always use sterile gloves, clean field, antiseptic skin prep
    - Simple interrupted: for most linear wounds – equal distance from edges, equal depth (dermal bites)
    - Mattress (horizontal/vertical): for everting edges in thick skin or wounds under tension
    - Continuous/over-and-over: for long linear wounds in low-tension areas (scalp, trunk) – quicker but risk of puckering
    - Use 4-0 or 5-0 (face/lid) monofilament nylon or polypropylene; 3-0 for high-tension areas (scalp, back)
    - Knot: instrument tie with 3-4 throws for braided, 4-5 for monofilament (non-absorbable)
  - Debride devitalised tissue if present
  - Apply steri-strips or tissue adhesive (e.g., 2-octylcyanoacrylate) for low-tension, clean wounds after suturing
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Keep wound dry for 24-48h (dressing)
  - Remove initial dressing after 24h to inspect wound; reapply clean dressing
  - If prophylactic antibiotics prescribed (e.g., for bites, contaminated wounds, immunocompromise): give first dose within 1 hour
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Suture removal timing (varies by site):
    - Face: 3-5 days
    - Scalp: 7-10 days
    - Trunk: 7-10 days
    - Limbs (non-weight bearing): 10-14 days
    - Palms/soles: 14-21 days
  - Advise patient about wound care, infection signs, and prevention of scarring (sun protection, silicone gel for high-risk scars)
- **Default orders (if admitted):**
  - Not applicable for simple suturing; patient discharged unless other reasons for admission.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Local anaesthetics:**
  - Lignocaine 1 % (plain): max dose 3 mg/kg (typically 200 mg in 70 kg adult) – use 1% = 10 mg/mL → 20 mL max
  - Lignocaine 1 % with adrenaline (1:200,000): max dose 7 mg/kg (500 mg in 70 kg) – 50 mL max
  - Bupivacaine 0.25% (with/without adrenaline): longer acting, cardiac toxicity – max 2 mg/kg (175 mg in 70 kg)
- **Antibiotics (prophylactic):**
  - Human/animal bite (high risk): co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS for 5 days (or 1.2 g IV TDS if severe)
  - If penicillin allergic: metronidazole 400 mg PO TDS + doxycycline 100 mg PO BD (or co-trimoxazole for animal bites)
  - Contaminated wound (soil, water): co-amoxiclav or clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS
  - Tetanus prophylaxis:
    - Wound type: Clean/minor – tetanus-prone (contaminated, devascularised) for risk assessment
    - UK schedule: 5 doses of tetanus vaccine in childhood → no booster unless >10 years since last dose and tetanus-prone wound
    - If unknown/incomplete: give tetanus vaccine + human tetanus immunoglobulin 250 units IM for tetanus-prone wounds
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Lignocaine with adrenaline: avoid in end arteries (digits, penis, nose, ear)
  - Co-amoxiclav: caution in hepatic impairment; check penicillin allergy history
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <10 mL/min – max 500/125 mg every 12 h)
  - Lignocaine: no dose adjustment for renal; hepatic impairment – cautious, reduce dose
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Lignocaine:** injecting > max dose (especially plain) → CNS toxicity (seizures, coma) then cardiac arrest. Always calculate total volume before infiltrating. Never use adrenaline in digits.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Wound infection (cellulitis, abscess): erythema, warmth, purulence – refer for incision and drainage if abscess
  - Wound dehiscence: sutures cut through or come undone – usually due to infection, tension, or premature removal
  - Haematoma: inadequate haemostasis during closure – evacuate if tense
  - Suture sinus/stitch abscess: localised inflammation around a suture
  - Nerve injury: anaesthesia, paraesthesia distal to wound (if suture catches nerve)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hypertrophic scar (raised, red, within wound margins)
  - Keloid scar (extending beyond wound margins)
  - Loss of sensation or motor function if nerve/tendon damaged during closure
sections.Prognosis: - Clean, well-approximated wound heals by primary intention in 7-14 days with minimal scarring. Infection rate <5% for clean wounds; up to 15-20% for contaminated or bite wounds.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always evert wound edges** – use forceps to lift skin edge and place suture with needle perpendicular to skin.
- **Use the smallest needle and suture necessary** – 5-0 or 6-0 on face, 4-0 on trunk, 3-0 on scalp.
- **Apply Steri-strips after suturing** to reduce wound tension and improve cosmetic outcome.
- **Document tetanus status, wound exploration findings, neurovascular check** – medicolegally vital.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not close a wound under tension** – it will dehisce; consider undermining edges or referral for closure.
- **Do not close a dirty or infected wound** – primary closure fails; debride, irrigate, leave open for delayed primary closure.
- **Do not forget to check for foreign body on XR** – missed glass/metal leads to chronic sinus and litigation.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing redness, warmth, swelling, or pus from wound
  - Fever >38°C
  - Wound edges separate or bleeding recurs
  - Numbness, tingling, or weakness distal to the wound
- **Follow-up:**
  - Wound check at 48-72 h for infected wounds
  - Suture removal at appropriate time (give written note with date)
  - GP practice or ED depending on local protocol
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar) if:**
  - Wound involves deep structures (tendon, nerve, vessel, bone, joint) – do not explore further
  - Wound over a major artery with pulsatile bleeding
  - Foreign body suspected but not visible on XR/ultrasound
  - Need for flap or complex reconstruction (e.g., facial laceration through vermillion border, eyelid margin, or ear)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (e.g., high temperature, tachycardia, hypotension – suggest sepsis)
  - Signs of compartment syndrome: pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia, pallor, pulselessness (rare from suturing but possible from tight dressing or haematoma)
  - Anaphylaxis to local anaesthetic (immediate adrenaline IM)

rawMarkdown:
## Suturing — Basic
**One-liner:** A practical skill for closing clean lacerations in ED/ward; primary intention healing reduces infection and scar; requires knowledge of technique, wound assessment, and timing.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Missed arterial injury:** "Pale, pulseless, cold, painful distal limb" — do not suture until you have confirmed distal pulse (Doppler) and no expanding haematoma.
- **Necrotising fasciitis:** "Crepitus + extreme pain out of proportion + systemic sepsis" — immediate surgical debridement, not closure.
- **Compound fracture or open joint:** "Visible bone, fat globules, air in joint on XR, or wound over a joint with hemarthrosis" — do not close; refer to orthopaedics.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible break in skin (laceration, incised wound, flap, puncture)
  - Bleeding (can be minimal or brisk)
  - Pain localised to wound
  - Mechanism: sharp (knife, glass, metal) vs blunt (tear, crush) vs bite (human/animal)
  - Time since injury (delayed closure >6h in non-facial wounds increases infection risk)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Wound location, length, depth, shape, edges (clean vs ragged)
  - Neurovascular status distal to wound: pulse, capillary refill, sensation (light touch, two-point discrimination), motor function (especially for hand/finger wounds)
  - Tendon function: test active flexion/extension against resistance
  - Foreign body: visible dirt, glass, metal; palpate for hard objects
  - Signs of infection at presentation: erythema, purulent discharge, lymphangitis

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators
  - **Simple superficial laceration:** skin only, no deep structure involvement, clean wound
  - **Deep laceration with tendon injury:** patient cannot actively flex/extend digit (e.g., zone II flexor tendon injury)
  - **Laceration with nerve injury:** sensory loss distal to wound (e.g., digital nerve injury in finger laceration)
  - **Laceration with vascular injury:** pulsatile bleeding, haematoma, distal ischaemia
  - **Open fracture or joint:** bone visible, fat globules, air track on XR; joint effusion with haemarthrosis
  - **Puncture wound:** small entry but may track deep; high risk of retained foreign body or deep infection (e.g., nail)
  - **Human/animal bite:** crush injury, high bacterial load, high risk of infection (e.g., clenched fist injury)
- **Splitter:** "Ability to fully extend the finger against resistance" – if present, flexor tendon injury is unlikely; if absent, immediate senior referral.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Wound irrigation with copious normal saline (at least 250-500 mL) – use 18G cannula and 50 mL syringe for high pressure
  - Explore wound with sterile probe/gauze – assess depth, check for foreign body or tendon exposure
  - Check tetanus immunisation status (UK Green Book schedule)
- **Bloods:**
  - Not routine for simple suturing
  - If signs of infection, severe wound, or immunocompromise: FBC, CRP, wound swab for MC&S
  - If human/animal bite or immunocompromised: consider HIV, Hep B/C (if high risk)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** X-ray two views (AP + lateral) for any wound with suspected foreign body (glass, metal, gravel) or possible fracture
  - **Second-line:** Ultrasound/CT for radiolucent foreign bodies (wood, plastic)
- **Special tests:**
  - Remove tourniquet if used for haemostasis before closure (risk of ischaemia)
  - For hand wounds: test flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) and superficialis (FDS)
- **Never-miss test:** X-ray for foreign body in any wound from glass, metal, or gravel – omission leads to retained foreign body and chronic infection.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Achieve haemostasis: direct pressure for 5 min, elevate, use tourniquet only temporarily
  - Clean wound: irrigate with saline, remove visible debris, consider 1% povidone-iodine for contaminated wounds (but avoid in clean wounds)
  - Anaesthesia: local infiltration with 1% lignocaine (max dose 200 mg plain, 500 mg with adrenaline – **[check local policy]**) – use adrenaline with caution in end arteries (fingers, toes, penis, ear, nose)
  - Suture technique:
    - Always use sterile gloves, clean field, antiseptic skin prep
    - Simple interrupted: for most linear wounds – equal distance from edges, equal depth (dermal bites)
    - Mattress (horizontal/vertical): for everting edges in thick skin or wounds under tension
    - Continuous/over-and-over: for long linear wounds in low-tension areas (scalp, trunk) – quicker but risk of puckering
    - Use 4-0 or 5-0 (face/lid) monofilament nylon or polypropylene; 3-0 for high-tension areas (scalp, back)
    - Knot: instrument tie with 3-4 throws for braided, 4-5 for monofilament (non-absorbable)
  - Debride devitalised tissue if present
  - Apply steri-strips or tissue adhesive (e.g., 2-octylcyanoacrylate) for low-tension, clean wounds after suturing
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Keep wound dry for 24-48h (dressing)
  - Remove initial dressing after 24h to inspect wound; reapply clean dressing
  - If prophylactic antibiotics prescribed (e.g., for bites, contaminated wounds, immunocompromise): give first dose within 1 hour
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Suture removal timing (varies by site):
    - Face: 3-5 days
    - Scalp: 7-10 days
    - Trunk: 7-10 days
    - Limbs (non-weight bearing): 10-14 days
    - Palms/soles: 14-21 days
  - Advise patient about wound care, infection signs, and prevention of scarring (sun protection, silicone gel for high-risk scars)
- **Default orders (if admitted):**
  - Not applicable for simple suturing; patient discharged unless other reasons for admission.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Local anaesthetics:**
  - Lignocaine 1 % (plain): max dose 3 mg/kg (typically 200 mg in 70 kg adult) – use 1% = 10 mg/mL → 20 mL max
  - Lignocaine 1 % with adrenaline (1:200,000): max dose 7 mg/kg (500 mg in 70 kg) – 50 mL max
  - Bupivacaine 0.25% (with/without adrenaline): longer acting, cardiac toxicity – max 2 mg/kg (175 mg in 70 kg)
- **Antibiotics (prophylactic):**
  - Human/animal bite (high risk): co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS for 5 days (or 1.2 g IV TDS if severe)
  - If penicillin allergic: metronidazole 400 mg PO TDS + doxycycline 100 mg PO BD (or co-trimoxazole for animal bites)
  - Contaminated wound (soil, water): co-amoxiclav or clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS
  - Tetanus prophylaxis:
    - Wound type: Clean/minor – tetanus-prone (contaminated, devascularised) for risk assessment
    - UK schedule: 5 doses of tetanus vaccine in childhood → no booster unless >10 years since last dose and tetanus-prone wound
    - If unknown/incomplete: give tetanus vaccine + human tetanus immunoglobulin 250 units IM for tetanus-prone wounds
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Lignocaine with adrenaline: avoid in end arteries (digits, penis, nose, ear)
  - Co-amoxiclav: caution in hepatic impairment; check penicillin allergy history
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <10 mL/min – max 500/125 mg every 12 h)
  - Lignocaine: no dose adjustment for renal; hepatic impairment – cautious, reduce dose
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Lignocaine:** injecting > max dose (especially plain) → CNS toxicity (seizures, coma) then cardiac arrest. Always calculate total volume before infiltrating. Never use adrenaline in digits.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Wound infection (cellulitis, abscess): erythema, warmth, purulence – refer for incision and drainage if abscess
  - Wound dehiscence: sutures cut through or come undone – usually due to infection, tension, or premature removal
  - Haematoma: inadequate haemostasis during closure – evacuate if tense
  - Suture sinus/stitch abscess: localised inflammation around a suture
  - Nerve injury: anaesthesia, paraesthesia distal to wound (if suture catches nerve)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hypertrophic scar (raised, red, within wound margins)
  - Keloid scar (extending beyond wound margins)
  - Loss of sensation or motor function if nerve/tendon damaged during closure

### Prognosis
- Clean, well-approximated wound heals by primary intention in 7-14 days with minimal scarring. Infection rate <5% for clean wounds; up to 15-20% for contaminated or bite wounds.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always evert wound edges** – use forceps to lift skin edge and place suture with needle perpendicular to skin.
- **Use the smallest needle and suture necessary** – 5-0 or 6-0 on face, 4-0 on trunk, 3-0 on scalp.
- **Apply Steri-strips after suturing** to reduce wound tension and improve cosmetic outcome.
- **Document tetanus status, wound exploration findings, neurovascular check** – medicolegally vital.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not close a wound under tension** – it will dehisce; consider undermining edges or referral for closure.
- **Do not close a dirty or infected wound** – primary closure fails; debride, irrigate, leave open for delayed primary closure.
- **Do not forget to check for foreign body on XR** – missed glass/metal leads to chronic sinus and litigation.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing redness, warmth, swelling, or pus from wound
  - Fever >38°C
  - Wound edges separate or bleeding recurs
  - Numbness, tingling, or weakness distal to the wound
- **Follow-up:**
  - Wound check at 48-72 h for infected wounds
  - Suture removal at appropriate time (give written note with date)
  - GP practice or ED depending on local protocol

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar) if:**
  - Wound involves deep structures (tendon, nerve, vessel, bone, joint) – do not explore further
  - Wound over a major artery with pulsatile bleeding
  - Foreign body suspected but not visible on XR/ultrasound
  - Need for flap or complex reconstruction (e.g., facial laceration through vermillion border, eyelid margin, or ear)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (e.g., high temperature, tachycardia, hypotension – suggest sepsis)
  - Signs of compartment syndrome: pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia, pallor, pulselessness (rare from suturing but possible from tight dressing or haematoma)
  - Anaphylaxis to local anaesthetic (immediate adrenaline IM)
